purpose: Starting orientation package for an agent exploring the playroom 3DGS scene. Gives a fixed standpoint, a defined North, a ring of eye-level views, and top-down plan views, all with exact camera coordinates. The agent may render additional/custom views by calling the render tool (see render_tool).

scene.ply_note: Recentered so the standpoint is the origin. Original un-centered scene is data/superspl/playroom.ply (this frame minus T, T=[1.5,-1.2,2.7]).

frame.north_definition: North is the -z direction: the forward look from the standpoint, facing the staircase/entry.

standpoint.eye_height_note: y=0 is standing eye level. Floor is ~3.2 below, ceiling ~2.3 above.

ring_views.description: 8 eye-level views from the standpoint, one per compass heading. All: eye [0,0,0], up [0,1,0], fov 90, look = 3 units toward heading.

views.name: north
views.heading: N
views.shows: staircase, under-stair door, yellow peanut beanbag

plan_views.description: Top-down floor plans. Both clip the ceiling/upper half at the standpoint plane (box ymax=0) and pan-center on the room footprint XZ=[0.5,0.0]. Plan-frame screen mapping: screen-right=+x (EAST), screen-down=+z (SOUTH), screen-up=-z (NORTH).

views.name: plan_ortho
views.note: High eye + narrow fov => straight walls, true footprint, minimal perspective.

views.name: plan_fov90
views.note: fov 90 forces a low eye; walls bow and furniture sides show. Not a true flat plan.

render_tool.usage: python view.py <heading | degrees | plan | plan90>  [fov=N] [from=x,y,z] [eye=x,y,z look=x,y,z]
notes: Simplest path: a heading name (n/ne/e/se/s/sw/w/nw) or a compass angle in degrees (0=N,90=E) aims from the standpoint at eye level, fov 90.
'plan' = clean near-orthographic floor plan; 'plan90' = fov-90 oblique plan. Ceiling clip is handled for you.
Pan by moving where you stand: from=+x is East/right, from=+z is South/down.
Lower fov= zooms in; higher zooms out.
Images save to views/ and print their path; each also writes a .json sidecar + a views/shots.csv row.
view.py wraps the underlying renderer ../shot.py; call shot.py directly only for exotic shots (custom --box/--sphere, HTML orbit viewer).
